fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small request-builder thing for the search service. Basically we want something that takes a high-level 'count documents' instruction and turns it into a proper HTTP request — method, path, params, body, all that. The tricky part (and the reason this came up) is that our ops team noticed the service is getting hit with a bunch of empty `{}` payloads on simple count requests, and some of the upstream servers are behaving weirdly when they receive those. So we need to make sure the body is only included when it actually has something meaningful in it — same kind of logic we used in that index-routing module a while back, you know the one.

Also some inputs are lightweight enough that they should just ride on the URL rather than going into a body. The structured stuff obviously needs to be serialized properly — for term-style queries, refer to how we nested things in the last search PR.

The whole thing needs to be cleanly separated — don't just throw everything in one file. There should be a runnable adapter that reads from stdin and prints the wire request to stdout so we can test it. Tests should live under rcb_tests/ and run with a single bash command. If something goes in wrong, don't blow up with a stack trace — surface a clean generic error. Probably a two-day task, let me know if anything is unclear.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for count, `method=POST` every time, full stop. Even if the request is totally empty and there are no params and no body, it still needs to come out as `method=POST`. Path-wise, if there’s no `index` at all, use `/_count`. If `index` is provided as an array, the path is the comma-joined version plus `/_count`, so `["logs", "events"]` becomes `/logs,events/_count`.

On the empty-payload issue, the rule is strict: include a body ONLY when the serialized request object has at least one property. If serialization ends up as `{}` because there wasn’t any structured query to put in there, then do not send a payload at all. In the adapter output that should show up as `body=absent`, not `body={}`. That stays true even if something like `q` is present, because `q` belongs on the query string and does not make the body count as present. More generally, the suppress-when-empty behavior should be its own reusable thing, decoupled from the endpoint-specific request building, same basic idea as keeping the `index` path handling generic instead of baking it into one special case.

Also confirming `q`: it is a lightweight query-string param, so it must be emitted as `query.q=<value>` and not serialized into the JSON body. It should follow the same `query.<name>=<value>` output format as the other query-string params, and those lines should be sorted alphabetically. Its presence has no effect on whether the body is included or suppressed.

For term queries, please transform the input shape instead of passing it through. The required body shape is `{"query":{"term":{"<field>":{"value":"<v>"}}}}`. So if field is `foo` and value is `bar`, the body is exactly `{"query":{"term":{"foo":{"value":"bar"}}}}`. The input shape `{"term": {"field": "foo", "value": "bar"}}` must be transformed — it is NOT passed through verbatim.

A few exact details to be precise about: When no collection/index is specified, the path is `/_count`. When one or more collections are named via the `index` field (an array), the path becomes `/<comma-joined-names>/_count` — e.g., for `["logs", "events"]` the path would be `/logs,events/_count`.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: CountRequest.build() -> WireRequest { method: String, path: String, queryParams: Map<String, String>, body: Optional<JsonBody> }

trigger_keywords: how to render the wire request
what lines does the adapter print
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter prints line-oriented text to stdout in this order: (1) `method=<HTTP method>`, (2) `path=<routed path>`, (3) one `query.<name>=<value>` line per query-string parameter, sorted alphabetically by name, (4) either `body=absent` if no payload is sent, or `body=<json>` with the exact JSON bytes. Each line ends with a newline. No extra metadata, PASS/FAIL summaries, or blank lines should be emitted.

trigger_keywords: what to do with bad input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any malformed or invalid input must be handled gracefully. The adapter must NOT leak stack traces or language-runtime error details to stdout. Instead it must emit a normalized, language-neutral error line such as `error=invalid_input`. This ensures the output contract is always clean and machine-readable regardless of what is passed in.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is invoked with `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It reads every `*.json` case file from `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` by default, or from a directory specified via `--cases-dir <subdir>`. For each case it writes the raw stdout from the program to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename.stem>@<case_index_zero_padded_3>.txt` — e.g., `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_1_no_body_for_empty_request@000.txt`. Output directories are namespaced by cases-dir so different runs never overwrite each other. Each `.txt` file contains only raw stdout — no PASS/FAIL annotations.

trigger_keywords: where should body decision logic live
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The body-inclusion decision (suppress-when-empty) must live in a reusable strategy component — not hard-coded at each call site. The core request-building logic (method, path, parameter, body resolution) must be physically separated from the stdin/stdout execution adapter. The adapter is solely responsible for parsing the JSON command and rendering output. Do not collapse everything into one file, but equally do not over-engineer a focused builder. The structure should reflect the actual complexity of the domain.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to how we nested things in the last search PR
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The term query serialization format: input shape `{"term": {"field": "foo", "value": "bar"}}` must be transformed to `{"query":{"term":{"foo":{"value":"bar"}}}}` — the field name becomes the key and the value is wrapped as `{"value": <v>}`.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll want to dig into the existing codebase or check with the engineering lead. The main things I care about from a business perspective are: no empty payloads going to the server, clean error messages (no stack traces), and a single bash command to run all tests.

feature1_1_no_body_for_empty_request.jso.description: When a document-count request carries no structured request body (either it is completely empty, or it only sets parameters that travel on the query string), the constructed wire request must contain NO HTTP request body. The adapter emits the HTTP method, the routed path, any query-string parameters (one `query.<name>=<value>` line, sorted by name), and a final `body=absent` line indicating the request was sent with no payload.

feature1_2_body_for_structured_query.jso.description: When a document-count request includes a structured query (e.g. a term query that matches a field against a value), that query must be serialized into a JSON HTTP request body. The adapter emits the HTTP method, the routed path, and a `body=<json>` line whose value is the exact JSON payload sent over the wire. A term query nests the matched value under the field name as `{"value": <v>}`.
